nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs; benthic dweller on rocks, sand, or rubble in marine and occasionally brackish coastal waters

diet.notes: An ambush predator that generally feeds on small fish and crustaceans by luring prey with a modified dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca). In captivity, live feeder shrimp are often needed initially; many individuals can be trained over time to accept frozen foods such as krill, mysis shrimp, and small pieces of fish. Feeding every 3–5 days is generally advised, as overfeeding has been reported to cause refusal of food.

goodWith: fish too large to be swallowed

summary: An unusual ambush predator known for its remarkable camouflage and lure-fishing behavior. Generally advised for intermediate aquarists; best kept singly and away from small fish or crustaceans that could be viewed as prey.
careNotes:
The Striated Tiger Colored Angler (Antennarius striatus) is a highly camouflaged ambush predator capable of altering its coloration to blend with its surroundings. It hunts by wiggling a modified dorsal spine tipped with a fleshy lure (illicium/esca) to attract small fish and crustaceans within striking range, then lunging forward and swallowing prey whole — including prey nearly equal to its own body size.

Temperature sources showed a notable spread: LiveAquaria reported 65–75°F (18.3–23.9°C), FishBase reported 18.2–28.6°C, TankFacts reported 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), and AquariumDomain reported 72–80°F (22.2–26.7°C). Averaged values used: 20.8–26.5°C. The lower end discrepancy (LiveAquaria's 65°F vs others' 76°F minimum) may reflect the species' broad wild range spanning temperate and tropical waters.

Minimum tank volume sources varied: LiveAquaria recommended 30 gallons (114 L), one secondary source recommended 50 gallons (189 L), and AquariumDomain recommended 55 gallons (208 L). The average of 170 L (~45 gallons) was used; the reviewer may wish to adjust upward for larger specimens.

Adult maximum size sources: LiveAquaria 8.5 in (21.6 cm), FishBase 25 cm, TankFacts 7 in (17.8 cm), AquariumDomain 9 in (22.9 cm); average maximum 22 cm used.

Care level: LiveAquaria rated this species 'easy', secondary sources rated it 'medium' to 'moderate'; an intermediate rating was applied, reflecting the feeding challenges (training to accept non-live foods) and prey-swallowing risk to tankmates.

This species possesses a venomous third dorsal spine that has been reported to inflict intense pain (comparable to a wasp sting) in humans — aquarists are advised to handle with appropriate caution.

Breeding in captivity is documented but considered uncommon; eggs are deposited in ribbon-like gelatinous masses. The species is generally considered reef-safe with respect to corals and sessile invertebrates, but has been known to consume any crustaceans or small fish within reach.
breedingNotes: Oviparous; wild individuals deposit eggs in ribbon-like gelatinous masses. Males have been reported to display more intense coloration and extended skin appendages during courtship. Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered rare and difficult to replicate intentionally.

additional.notes: Additional cross-check on temperature (72–80°F / 22.2–26.7°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (55 gal / 208 L), and adult size (9 in / 22.9 cm). Used for averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; captive breeding is documented but uncommon. Eggs released in ribbon-like gelatinous masses. Males display intensified coloration during courtship.
